module: Module 2: In Search of a Universal Theory
content: So today in this module, we will now take forward Marx's ideas and look at a contemporary application of Marx's ideas. This will help us to understand how these ideas are being used today. And it'll give you examples of concepts like base and superstructure, the different forms of alienation, surplus and class struggles. So with this purpose in mind today, we are going to look at this work by Barbara Ehrenreich called Nickel and Dimed. This was written in 2001, so this is a contemporary piece. Now very briefly, who was Barbara Ehrenreich. She is a contemporary of ours. She passed away in 2022. She was born in 1941 in a blue-collar mining town. Her father was a copper miner who later became a senior executive at the Gillette Corporation. So you can see that, you know, biography, going back to Mill's Ideas. Her biography, her background in a blue-collar mining town with her father as a blue-collar worker. That definitely has had an influence on the later work that she has done. So this goes back to Mills' ideas of how biography impacts history. She attended Reed College, majoring in physics, chemistry and then went on to get a PhD in cellular immunology. So this is very different from the final work that she ended up doing. And how did this shift happen? After the birth of her first child, she underwent a really big personal and professional transformation because of her experiences giving birth she became very involved in the women's health movement and then also the issues of labor and Labor movement. She's best known for this book, and you have all, it's a required reading. You've read an excerpt from this book called Nickel and Dimed and this book, it's her most famous work. And it was written in this context where in the US at the time in the 1990s, there was this big debate going on and it was based on sort of this, contrasting view of work. So the belief was that there, there were too many unemployed people and there were too many unemployment and other benefits being given to these people and because you had so many benefits, people didn't want to work. They were becoming lazier and lazier. So the idea was that these poor people who the government was giving benefits too. They could have easily escaped poverty if they simply chose to work and they were not choosing to work because of all these benefits, and that's why now society was having to support them. So her goal was to determine if this is actually true. If a full-time, low wage job in America could actually cover the basic cost of living rent, food, healthcare, so that was her objective in this work. Now, for this work, she was doing this kind of social experiment and she imposed on herself 3 rules. The first was that she had to take the highest paying job that she could find in the local area. The second is she had to take any job offered without relying on any of her prior professional contacts or any of her prior skills. Now remember, she had a PhD, but she is now applying for labor intensive, low skilled jobs, and she had to use her actual earnings from these jobs for all her expenses, housing, food, transport, everything. She could not use credit cards or she could not rely on her own personal previous savings. So this was the experiment that she had decided to do. Now, what was the core question? The core question was, can a person survive and maintain basic human dignity, working full-time at minimum or low wage jobs in America? Now, remember, America is supposed to be one of the richest countries in the world. So the question is if you work full-time in America at a minimum or low wage job, can you maintain basic human dignity and can you maintain yourself and survive? That's the question. Now you've all read the paper. So what I would like to do in this section and video is I would like to discuss how Ehrenreich work helps us to understand Marxist's ideas. And specifically, which ideas will this work allow us to understand? One is the idea of surplus value and class war. Second is the different forms of alienation. The third is the idea of base and superstructure. Finally, the idea of societal instability and possibly revolution. So these are the four ideas that we'll use  Ehrenreich's work to understand these ideas better. So first concept, surplus value, and 2 classes. So in Ehrenreich's work, we see the application of this idea of surplus value, class wars and minimum living wage. So what is surplus value? The difference between the value a worker creates and the wage they receive is called surplus value according to Marx. And this surplus value, according to Marx, is the source of the capitalist profit. So in Ehrenreich's case, she's getting $2.43 dollars per hour. So this is the wage she's being paid. The idea is that she's also making tips and that's how she can manage to survive. But the wage itself is $2.43 per hour, and whatever surplus now is created as a profit from this fast food restaurant. Above and beyond this wage and other costs goes to the owner, the bourgeoisie, according to Marx's ideas. Now, the problem is that because of competition between bourgeois. Especially because the small bourgeoisie, the small, you know, restaurant owners, they're competing also with a large bourgeoisie with large capital. So big chains like McDonald's, you know, which are everywhere, right? So these small owners are also competing with the large bourgeoisie. Now, because of this constant competition, you are as an owner, continuously reducing. The wages because your margins are reducing and so you have a continuous pressure to keep decreasing wages because otherwise you as a business will not survive. So now you keep reducing until you reach a wage. That is the bare minimum for this worker to survive because you need the worker, right. Otherwise you cannot run this business. So you keep reducing until you reach a bare minimum for the worker to survive. So that's the question and Ehrenreich discovers that it, this wage, this minimum wage is not sufficient for her to survive. And so she needs 2 jobs just to be able to eat and pay rent, and most of the other workers that she's working for with also have the same situation. So this is an example here of Marx's prediction about how capitalism creates a system, where it destroys itself because it cannot sustain the survival of the very people on whom surplus that is capital is based. Okay, so that was the first application we see in Ehrenreich contemporary application of Marxist ideas of surplus and class struggle. Now we let's look at other concepts that we discussed in Marx. Marx talks about different types of alienation. The first type of alienation is alienation from the product. So you as a worker, you don't have right to the product that you have created. So for instance, if I'm in a factory creating a semiconductor, I don't own that semiconductor, right? I just get a wage. So in Ehrenreich case she serves these huge calorie laden meals, fudge desserts, fried steaks to the customers. But she herself, at the end of the day, she's hungry and servers like her are not allowed to eat at the restaurant where they are serving the food and part of the production process for that food. So this is an example of Marxist's idea of alienation from the product. So what is Marx saying, you have no right to the product itself that you are helping create. You also have no right to the surplus generated by the product. Now, the surplus that is a profit goes to the owner, and this owner never appears in the reading that you have. They Ehrenreich never even meets this person. They never come to the restaurant. So she gets wage and which is not enough for her to survive, but the surplus is going to someone who on the face of it, is not actually involved in the production of this product, which is this fast food. So now what happens in the rest of the excerpt, you know, that she cannot survive with one job. So now she takes up two jobs. So she's working now from 8:00 AM to 10:00 PM every day. And this is, you know, this is not a nice desk job. We are talking about very labor intensive work, which involves standing for hours at a time. And she is kind of my age. You know, even at the age of 18, that's difficult but by the time you reach 45-50, this actually is very painful. And in Ehrenreich's case, she is in intense pain for her intense back pain. So she starts taking ibuprofen before every shift just to get through the shift so that's an example of how of the I of Marxist's idea of she has no right to the surplus generated by the product. Now let's look at another of Marx's concepts, which is alienation from the process of labor. So the job that she is doing is non-creative, repetitive, and subject to strong external control. She doesn't have control over what she does. So, for instance the job of the waitress has been sort of dumbed down. All the skilled part is deemphasized or not compensated. So it's broken down into this mechanical routines of sweeping, scrubbing, refilling, carrying multiple orders, serving, communicating with the chef and the other kitchen team. So whatever part of the job, which requires you to express your own unique skills and talents or personality. So for instance, interaction with the customers knowing your regular customers and talking to them helping them to understand if they have any questions about the menu. All of these are actually deemphasized and in fact, the manager tells her that you are spending too much time on doing this at one point, right. So all of those skilled parts, because in a way, waitressing, like in any service profession, it's emotional labor. You are at some level connecting with this other human being and serving them. So we see in Ehrenreich is exactly what Marx predicts, which is that all of these skills are deemphasized. And the only thing that the hiring managers care about is have you committed any felonies and are you a legal resident of the US? That's it. They don't want to look at what are your skills? What is your previous work experience? Nothing. Because the job has become like a commodity. The worker itself has become like a commodity. There is no difference really between one worker and the next because the job has been de-skilled, the skill element of the job has been de-emphasized. But as she discovers, there is definitely skill involved and the other workers out of kindness and Marx would call it solidarity, keep helping her so that she's able to do this. And so this person who has a PhD feels that the biggest issue is that she misses the feeling of competence because she doesn't have the skills needed to do this pretty demanding job. So in addition to this emotional labor of caring for her customers, serving her customers who are themselves, working class people, for her, she's troubled by, you know, messing up that a table does not get the order on time, or a child at the table gets the order late after everyone else has finished and she cares about it. As a worker, but the management, in fact tries to deemphasize this type of, this type of emotional labor that is a more skilled element of her interaction with her customers. So that is another form of alienation. Alienation from the process of labor. Now let's discuss a third form of alienation, which Marx?s talks about, which is alienation from your fellow workers. So for example, in Ehrenreich, we see that management has a ban on gossip. And gossip is defined as what any kind of talking between workers is looked down upon. Management is suspicious of it or off duty employees sitting together and eating in the restaurant and all of these are ways in which there is the solidarity being built. And Marx will say, this is how proletarian solidarity gets built, because they're all in this together and the management doesn't want that solidarity to get built. So these small acts of kindness between the workers where your covering for a sick colleague, you're helping them do their job, you're training them, you're sharing tips. These are all from the management's perspective, becomes acts of resistance. And the interesting part is that the management here they themselves are wage workers, right. The owners never appear in the whole excerpt. So the managers are themselves people who probably don't have health insurance and are wage workers. But the system creates this antagonistic relationship and because the management has to implement all of these rules. So for instance, if a manager catches you in an idle moment, he will give you something far nastier to do, Ehrenreich makes this comment. So you should never just sit around that is basically the logic. You have to be constantly working because you are being paid by the hour. There is a ban on gossip, talking among workers, lockers can be searched at any time. At any time, you can be asked to pee in a cup to do a urine test. There is a no eating rule, so you know, while you are at work you cannot, any site of food, even though this is a fast food restaurant, is very offensive and against the rules. Now we can look at all this and think, oh, this is the US. You know, this doesn't apply to us, but I would encourage you to think and look all around you. One of the examples Ehrenreich gives is that you know, she's not called by her name. People call her baby or girl, or Blondie or honey. So she, she loses her individuality to the extent that people don't even call her her name. Now think about the Indian context before you dismiss all this by saying, oh, this is only in America. Have you heard the word chotu? So, even a 30-year-old man, because he is working in the small store, he is called show to by everybody. So he loses his name or think of the person who does jhaadoo pochha in your house. The lady who comes to your house and cleans. At the gate, their bags can be checked randomly, or they're asked to keep their bags outside. The idea, the assumption is that they will steal. So very similar random checks locker checks in Ehrenreich's case. And, you know, you might have seen people saying you know, the jhaadoo pochha whoever's doing, you cannot sit. If, she's sitting, that means she doesn't have work. So give her some work. If she's a full-time person in your house and there's always these comment, why do you, why do they eat so much? I mean, they're doing manual labor, so they need to eat, right. And there's this ban, unsaid ban against why, why is a cleaning lady talking to the gardener or the cook? Because they might be plotting against you. They might be gossiping. So very similar, if we look around us, we will see that, this is not a peculiarly American problem. This is a social problem that spans all societies. So another form of alienation that Marx talks about is alienation from the self, from your species being where the worker is reduced to the animal function. Another example from Ehrenreich is of the concept from Marx, which is called alienation, from your species being, where Marx talks about how the worker is reduced to their basic animal function and so you feel human only during your break types, not while you're working. When there is a break is when you get to relax. And Ehrenreich mentioned something similar when during the break is when they get to relax. Finally, let's look at Marx's idea of base and superstructure. So what is Marx's, let's just remind ourselves, what is Marx's idea of base and superstructure? The base is the economic mode of production, the material conditions of production, so instruments of production, relations of production, so that's the base, the foundation. According to Marx, everything else is a superstructure, law, religion, culture, morality, art, literature all of that is superstructure. The purpose of which is just to legitimize the base, right. So we see that when she talks about the worst customers for her are the Christian customers who have just come from church. They don't tip and they look down on her acting as if she's doing something akin to prostitution. Because this the, there is this morality where the idea is that if you are in a low wage job, it must be because you are lazy or some kind of drug addict, or you might be somehow morally deficient and so they look down on her and they feel justified in not tipping her well. So you have this kind of, this moral ideas justifying exploitation. And you know, Ehrenreich is talking from the perspective of American society and so Christianity happens to be the dominant religion of the people in the US but before we feel superior, just think about our, so society, all societies, in fact. Have some kind of value system today that supports capitalistic exploitation and this is what Marx would say. So for instance religious and moral ideas that if somebody is poor, it must be because they have done something wrong in this birth or the last birth. And so they deserve this and so the exploitation is acceptable and we may not do anything about it. So these are, according to Marx, a superstructure built to justify the base, the economic conditions, and the relations, the economic relations between people. Final prediction of Marx, societal instability and revolution. So Ehrenreich shows us how these micro daily struggles of minimum wage workers and helps us to understand how this is a system that drives large populations into misery where they're not able to maintain daily existence. Now, this type of system where the majority is in this situation, it creates solidarity, but it also creates profound instability in the system itself. Businesses cannot really operate successfully in a society which is riddled with chronic poverty, poor health, stress and uncertainty. In addition, this kind of instability, and then you are creating the conditions where solidarity can form automatically. This will, according to Marx, not just threaten the market itself and the capitalist system, it'll lead to revolution according to Marx, perhaps violent armed revolution, yeah. So whether you agree with Marx or not, what I would like you to get out of this this section on Marx is how you can understand the society from the perspective of historical materialism, which we discussed in the last section, how society evolves based on these kind of the the mode of production changing. So that's one. And the second, how the base and superstructure works in every era, in every type of system, whether it was the feudal system or the capitalist system or any other system, whatever is the economic relations and the mode of production, there is that base, but then there is an ideology and a morality and art and literature and political systems which come up to legitimize and to keep that base stable. So we saw that in Ehrenreich in her example of the how the base and superstructure plays out.
